dailyJudgementSnippet: Google Gemini narrowly led Hacker News sentiment on 2026-04-07 by being the only tracked company with a net positive score, helped by praise for Gemini 3.1 Pro and Gemini ER’s reasoning capabilities [E12][E9]. OpenAI was mixed but slightly negative, while Anthropic and Microsoft Copilot faced more sustained criticism, especially Anthropic around product reliability and customer treatment [E5][E8].
winnerExplanation: Google Gemini won because it posted the strongest aggregate sentiment score of the four at 0.0316, and it was the only entity above neutral. The positive side was driven by direct praise for Gemini ER’s flexible programming and reasoning [E9] and for Gemini 3.1 Pro being 'significantly better' and worth paying for [E12]. There were negatives tied to Google AI Overviews being inaccurate or seemingly powered by a poor Gemini variant [E10][E11], but those criticisms were not enough to outweigh the positives overall. OpenAI finished second at -0.0972: discussion was mixed, with some users strongly preferring GPT for complex codebases and saying OpenAI-based review workflows worked very well [E2][E4], but those positives were offset by criticism of model value/performance and broader hostility toward the company’s leadership and mission [E1][E3]. Anthropic was clearly weaker at -0.3543 despite the largest volume of discussion, with multiple strong negative drivers focused on Claude regressions, reliability, pricing, support, and accusations that the company was alienating paying customers [E5][E6][E8]; criticism of the CEO also added to the drag [E7]. Microsoft Copilot also remained negative at -0.2775, as praise for VS Code integration and value [E15] was outweighed by complaints about login flow, weak product integration, usability problems, and the sense that the Windows Copilot app was not a meaningful standalone experience [E13][E14][E16].

openai.judgementSnippet: OpenAI landed in mixed territory but slightly negative overall: there was meaningful praise for GPT on large codebases and for OpenAI-based review setups [E2][E4], yet criticism of performance, pricing, and company conduct kept sentiment below zero [E1][E3].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Anthropic had the roughest day in practical terms because criticism was broad and repeated across product quality, support, pricing, trust, and customer treatment [E5][E6][E8], with additional reputational pressure from backlash to the CEO’s claims [E7].

google_gemini.judgementSnippet: Google Gemini came out on top because positive commentary about Gemini model capability and quality [E9][E12] slightly outweighed negative reactions to AI Overviews accuracy [E10][E11], leaving it as the only net-positive entity.

microsoft_copilot.judgementSnippet: Microsoft Copilot stayed negative overall: one strong endorsement for VS Code use and value [E15] was outweighed by complaints about rollout, integration, and usability, plus mockery of the Windows app experience [E13][E14][E16].

evidence.summary: Directly criticizes OpenAI model performance and value, saying it is no better than prior versions, often worse than open-weight models, and not competitive due to high price and low-ish quality.
The commenter says they use GPT 5.4 exclusively and finds it better than Claude and other models, especially for large and complex codebases.

The comment directly accuses OpenAI/Sam Altman of lying about the mission, destroying the nonprofit structure, causing layoffs, and raising power bills through datacenter demand, with a clearly hostile overall judgment.
Direct praise for GPT/OpenAI as a reviewer: the comment says this setup "works extremely well."

The post is broadly and repeatedly critical of Anthropic’s company strategy, support experience, product reliability, pricing, and dogfooding. It says Anthropic tells others to use AI internally but does not do so itself, describes broken Claude Code/Desktop behavior, complains about lack of SLA/credits and usage charges, and frames Anthropic as profiting from poor support.
The story directly attributes a regression in Claude to Anthropic’s model quality and says it cannot be trusted for complex engineering, which is strongly negative toward Anthropic.
The story frames Anthropic’s CEO as being criticized by doctors for allegedly spreading false information about AI replacing radiologists, which is directly negative toward Anthropic.
The story title directly accuses Anthropic of 'pushing away its paying customers,' which is a clear negative judgment of the company’s strategy and treatment of customers.

The story explicitly credits Gemini ER with flexible task programming and reasoning, and says the system retains the semantic reasoning of a foundation AI model. This is direct praise of Gemini’s model capability, not just the surrounding robotics pipeline.

The story headline directly condemns Google's AI Overviews as highly inaccurate, implying a strong negative judgment of Google's AI-generated answer product. This is relevant to Google Gemini only insofar as it reflects Google's AI output quality.
The commenter directly criticizes Google’s AI Overviews as wrong and says they seem to use a very bad version of Gemini, while also contrasting it unfavorably with AI Mode.
Explicitly praises Gemini 3.1 Pro as significantly better and recommends paying for it if needed.
The comment criticizes Copilot's login flow, lack of integration, and overall rollout, calling its connected experiences 'just a chat window' and implying it is inferior to Claude.
The item frames the Windows 11 Copilot app as essentially Microsoft Edge, implying it is not a distinct or worthwhile Copilot product experience.
The commenter says Copilot is their main tool, praises its VS Code integration for existing codebases, and calls it a great value for the price and usage.
The comment directly criticizes Copilot’s usability: the user is forced to use it at work, cannot resize the font, sees input lag, and says Outlook opens the side panel unexpectedly.
